flashcards.definition: The ability to understand and effectively use financial skills including budgeting, investing, and debt management.
Managing individual or family financial activities: income, spending, saving, investing, and protection.
Interest earned on both principal and accumulated interest — Einstein reportedly called it the 'eighth wonder of the world.'
The rate at which prices increase over time, reducing the purchasing power of money (~2-3% annually on average).
Total assets minus total liabilities — the fundamental measure of financial health.
Money received from labor: wages, salary, tips, commissions, and bonuses.
Income requiring little active effort: rental income, royalties, dividends from investments.
Total earnings before any deductions (taxes, insurance, retirement contributions).
Take-home pay after all deductions — the money actually available to spend or save.
Profit from selling an asset (stock, property) for more than you paid for it.
A financial plan allocating income to expenses, savings, and investments over a specific period.
Budget guideline: 50% needs, 30% wants, 20% savings and debt repayment.
Budget where every dollar of income is assigned a specific purpose (income minus all allocations = $0).
Costs that stay the same each period: rent/mortgage, car payment, insurance premiums.
Costs that fluctuate: groceries, utilities, entertainment, clothing.
Essential expenses required for survival and basic functioning: food, shelter, healthcare, basic clothing, transportation.
Non-essential expenses that enhance quality of life: entertainment, dining out, luxury items, vacations.
The value of the next-best alternative given up when making a choice.
Increasing spending as income rises — upgrading wants to feel like needs.
Choosing to wait for a better outcome rather than taking an immediate, smaller reward.
The value of the next-best alternative given up when a choice is made.
A situation where gaining something requires giving up something else.
Money already spent that cannot be recovered — should NOT influence future decisions.
Comparing the additional benefit vs. additional cost of a decision.
Systematic comparison of all costs and benefits of a decision to determine the best option.
Financial goals that are Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound.
Savings covering 3–6 months of essential expenses for unexpected events (job loss, medical emergency).
Having sufficient passive income or savings to cover living expenses without active employment.
Initial upfront payment when purchasing an asset (typically 3–20% for a home).
Investing fixed amounts at regular intervals regardless of market price — reduces timing risk.
Starting to invest 10 years earlier with less total money can yield MORE than investing 3× more starting later, due to compound interest.
When minimum payments mostly cover interest, causing debt to take decades to pay off and cost multiples of the original amount.
Making only minimum credit card payments extends debt repayment for decades and dramatically increases total cost.
Debt avalanche (highest interest first) saves the most money; debt snowball (smallest balance first) provides psychological wins.
The more frequently interest compounds (daily vs. monthly vs. annually), the more total interest accumulated.
Systematic approach: identify → gather info → list alternatives → evaluate → decide → review.
Study of how psychological biases affect financial decisions (overconfidence, loss aversion, herd behavior).
The tendency to feel losses more strongly than equivalent gains — losing $100 hurts more than gaining $100 feels good.
Over-relying on the first piece of information encountered when making decisions (e.g., original price as anchor).
Tool for comparing options by scoring each against weighted criteria to identify the best choice.
Interest calculated only on the original principal: I = P × r × t.
The original amount of money deposited or borrowed before interest.
The percentage charged or earned per year, expressed as a decimal in formulas (e.g., 5% = 0.05).
Duration in years for the interest calculation (convert months with t = months ÷ 12).
Principal plus interest: A = P(1 + rt) for simple interest.
Interest calculated on principal plus all previously accumulated interest: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt).
How often interest is calculated per year: annually (1), semi-annually (2), quarterly (4), monthly (12), daily (365).
The effective annual rate accounting for compounding — always ≥ APR for the same nominal rate.
Each interval after which interest is calculated and added to the balance.
The value of an investment at a specific future date, accounting for compound interest growth.
Interest compounded infinitely often: A = Pe^(rt), where e ≈ 2.71828.
Mathematical constant ≈ 2.71828; base of the natural logarithm; limit of (1+1/n)^n as n→∞.
The inverse of e^x; used to solve for time or rate in continuous compounding: t = ln(A/P)/r.
The rate at which a quantity grows when modeled with continuous compounding.
As compounding frequency n→∞, A = P(1+r/n)^(nt) approaches A = Pe^(rt).
Amount increases by a fixed percentage each period: A = P(1+r)^t (investments, savings).
Amount decreases by a fixed percentage each period: A = P(1−r)^t (depreciation, purchasing power).
The decrease in value of an asset over time (cars, equipment, buildings) — typically modeled as exponential decay.
What a dollar can actually buy — decreases over time due to inflation (decay of money's value).
Time for an asset to lose half its value: t = ln(0.5)/ln(1−r).
Online savings accounts offering significantly higher APY (~4–5%) than traditional bank accounts.
Time deposit earning a fixed interest rate for a fixed term; early withdrawal incurs a penalty.
Savings account offering higher interest than regular savings, often with limited check-writing.
Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation protects bank deposits up to $250,000 per depositor per institution.
APY includes compounding (what you earn); APR does not (what you're charged on loans).
Monthly compounding yields more than annual at the same nominal rate; difference grows with time and rate.
Small differences in interest rates create enormous differences in outcomes over long periods.
More time dramatically increases compound interest — the most powerful variable in wealth building.
The actual annual rate after accounting for compounding; used to compare different compounding frequencies.
Present value: what a future amount is worth today; Future value: what today's money will be worth in the future.
Online banks offer 10-100× higher savings rates than traditional banks due to lower overhead costs.
Strategy of dividing deposits across CDs with staggered maturity dates to balance rate and access.
Keeping money in a 0.05% account vs. 4.5% on $25,000 costs ~$1,112/year in lost earnings.
Maintenance fees, overdraft fees, ATM fees — can erode returns if not managed (choose fee-free options).
Consistent savings + compound interest over 40+ years builds substantial wealth from modest contributions.
Spreadsheet function calculating future value: =FV(rate, nper, pmt, pv) — returns future value of investment.
Spreadsheet function calculating present value: =PV(rate, nper, pmt, fv) — what a future amount is worth today.
Spreadsheet function calculating payment: =PMT(rate, nper, pv) — regular payment needed to reach a goal.
Time Value of Money: N (periods), I/Y (interest/year), PV (present value), PMT (payment), FV (future value).
Using technology to build what-if scenarios comparing different financial options and assumptions.
The original amount of money borrowed in a loan.
The true annual cost of a loan, including interest and fees — required by law for disclosure.
An asset pledged to secure a loan; lender can seize it if you default (e.g., home, car).
A loan backed by collateral — typically lower interest rates because the lender has less risk.
The process of paying off a loan through regular scheduled payments over time (principal + interest).
Table showing each payment's split between principal reduction and interest expense over the life of a loan.
PMT = P × r(1+r)^n / [(1+r)^n − 1] where r = monthly rate and n = total payments.
Early loan payments go mostly toward interest; principal reduction accelerates in later payments.
Additional payments applied to principal reduce total interest and shorten the loan term significantly.
After each payment, the outstanding principal = previous balance − principal portion of that payment.
Total monthly mortgage cost: Principal + Interest + Taxes + Insurance.
Housing costs should not exceed 28% of gross monthly income for affordability.
One-time fees at purchase: typically 2–5% of loan amount (appraisal, title, origination, etc.).
Account held by lender to pay property taxes and insurance from your monthly payment.
Upfront fees paid to reduce the interest rate; 1 point = 1% of the loan amount.
Annual Percentage Rate on a credit card, typically 15–25%; applied daily to the outstanding balance.
Paying only the minimum extends repayment to decades and costs many times the original purchase.
~25 days after the billing cycle ends where no interest accrues if you pay in full each month.
Moving debt from one card to another (often at a promotional 0% rate) to save on interest.
The percentage of your available credit that you're using; keeping it < 30% helps your credit score.
Federal student loan where the government pays interest while you're in school at least half-time.
Federal student loan where interest accrues from disbursement — even while in school.
Unpaid interest that is added to the principal balance, increasing the amount on which future interest is calculated.
Plans that cap monthly payments at a percentage (10-20%) of discretionary income.
Programs that cancel remaining student loan balance after meeting certain conditions (e.g., PSLF after 10 years of public service).
Duration of car financing; longer terms = lower payments but more interest + risk of being underwater.
Owing more on a loan than the asset is worth — common with long auto loans due to rapid depreciation.
Capitalization Rate = Net Operating Income / Property Value; measures investment property return.
Rental income minus all expenses (mortgage, taxes, insurance, maintenance, vacancy).
The portion of the property you own: market value − remaining mortgage balance.
Pay off highest-interest debt first while making minimum payments on others — mathematically optimal strategy.
Pay off smallest balance first for psychological wins; mathematically costs more but motivates many people.
Combining multiple debts into a single loan at a lower interest rate to simplify and reduce cost.
Total monthly debt payments ÷ gross monthly income; lenders use this to assess borrowing capacity.
Structured approach to eliminating debt: assess all debts, choose a strategy, automate payments, build emergency fund.
Sum of all payments − principal = total interest; the true measure of borrowing cost.
Compare loans by APR (not just interest rate) because APR includes fees for a more complete picture.
If loan rate < potential investment return, extra money may be better invested than used for extra payments.
Loan amount ÷ property value; lower LTV = less risk = better terms from lenders.
Calculate how long it takes for savings from a financial decision (e.g., refinancing) to exceed the costs.
A share of ownership in a company; returns come from dividends and price appreciation (capital gains).
A loan to a government or corporation; pays regular interest (coupon) and returns face value at maturity.
A pooled investment fund managed by a professional that holds a diversified portfolio of stocks, bonds, or both.
Similar to a mutual fund but trades on an exchange like a stock; often tracks an index with low fees.
A portion of a company's profits distributed to shareholders, typically quarterly.
Higher expected returns require accepting higher risk — there is no free lunch in investing.
Statistical measure of volatility; higher SD = wider range of possible outcomes (more risk).
The degree of variation in investment returns — high volatility means large price swings.
An individual's ability and willingness to endure investment losses; depends on time horizon, income, and temperament.
Measure of a stock's volatility relative to the overall market; beta > 1 means more volatile than the market.
A series of equal payments made at regular intervals (monthly, quarterly, annually).
Future value of regular payments: FV = PMT × [(1+r)^n − 1] / r.
Present value of future payment stream: PV = PMT × [1 − (1+r)^(−n)] / r.
Payments made at the BEGINNING of each period (vs. ordinary annuity at end); FV is slightly higher.
Regular retirement income guaranteed by an employer; value calculated as present value of future annuity payments.
Employer-sponsored retirement plan with pre-tax contributions; many employers offer matching contributions.
Individual retirement account funded with after-tax dollars; all growth and qualified withdrawals are tax-free.
Individual retirement account with tax-deductible contributions; withdrawals in retirement are taxed as income.
Employer contributes to your 401(k) based on your contributions (e.g., 50% match up to 6% = instant 50% return).
Mandatory annual withdrawals from traditional retirement accounts starting at age 73.
Strategy of purchasing and holding investments long-term regardless of short-term market volatility.
Investing a fixed dollar amount at regular intervals; automatically buys more shares when prices are low.
Periodically selling winners and buying laggards to maintain your target asset allocation.
Selling investments at a loss to offset capital gains, reducing your tax bill.
The percentage split between stocks, bonds, and cash — the most important investment decision.
Spreading investments across asset classes, sectors, and geographies to reduce risk.
Statistical measure (−1 to +1) of how two assets move relative to each other; lower = better diversification.
Framework showing how to construct portfolios that maximize expected return for a given risk level.
Curve showing optimal portfolios that offer the best expected return for each level of risk.
Market risk (can't be diversified away) vs. company-specific risk (can be diversified away).
Investing all at once (lump sum) historically beats DCA ~66% of the time, but DCA reduces timing risk.
Starting 10 years earlier can outperform even 3× longer investing periods due to compounding.
Emotional reactions (panic selling, FOMO buying) are the biggest destroyers of investment returns.
Markets have recovered from every crash in history; staying invested during downturns is critical.
Even 1% in annual fees can cost hundreds of thousands over a career due to compounding.
Automated investment platform that builds and manages a diversified portfolio based on your goals and risk tolerance.
Tool for filtering stocks based on criteria: market cap, P/E ratio, dividend yield, sector, etc.
Computer model running thousands of random scenarios to estimate the probability of investment outcomes.
Testing an investment strategy against historical data to see how it would have performed in the past.
Application Programming Interface for accessing real-time and historical financial data programmatically.
A plan for how income will be spent, saved, and invested over a specific time period.
Budgeting guideline: 50% needs, 30% wants, 20% savings/debt repayment.
Costs that stay the same each month (rent, loan payments, insurance premiums).
Costs that fluctuate monthly (groceries, utilities, entertainment).
Income minus expenses: positive = surplus (save/invest); negative = deficit (spending more than earning).
Total earnings before any deductions (taxes, insurance, retirement contributions).
Take-home pay after all deductions — the actual amount available for budgeting.
The percentage of income spent on each category; helps identify areas for adjustment.
Regular, predictable expenses (subscriptions, memberships, loan payments).
Recording all spending to compare actual vs. budgeted amounts and identify patterns.
3–6 months of essential expenses saved in a liquid, accessible account for unexpected major expenses.
A specific financial target with a timeline and required monthly contribution to achieve it.
How quickly an asset can be converted to cash without significant loss — emergency funds need high liquidity.
Money saved incrementally for a specific planned future expense (vacation, car down payment, wedding).
Cash reserve that prevents small emergencies from becoming financial disasters or debt spirals.
The process of setting goals, assessing resources, and creating strategies to achieve financial objectives.
The expected time period until a financial goal is needed; determines risk tolerance and investment choices.
Total assets minus total liabilities; the comprehensive measure of financial health.
Key target on the path to a larger goal (e.g., paying off credit cards before attacking student loans).
What you give up when choosing one option over another (e.g., spending now vs. investing for later).
How income is distributed across expense categories — reveals financial priorities and potential imbalances.
Percentage of income saved: a 12-15% rate is healthy; 20%+ accelerates wealth building.
Housing expenses ÷ income; should stay below 30% for financial stability.
Intentionally choosing spending priorities based on values, not defaults or social pressure.
Building in 'flex' or buffer money for unexpected expenses within the budget framework.
You Need A Budget — zero-based budgeting app that assigns every dollar a specific purpose.
Technology that automatically classifies transactions into spending categories (food, transport, etc.).
Feature that rounds purchases to the nearest dollar and saves the difference (e.g., $3.75 purchase → $0.25 saved).
Notifications triggered when approaching or exceeding budget category limits.
Technology-generated forecast of future income and expenses based on historical patterns.
The amount needed to cover basic expenses in a particular area; varies dramatically by region.
Financial comparison of renting vs. purchasing a home, considering down payment, maintenance, equity, and market conditions.
Purchase price + insurance + fuel + maintenance + depreciation + registration — the TRUE cost of car ownership.
The financial return foregone by choosing one lifestyle option over another.
Living in a lower-cost area while earning income at higher-cost area rates (e.g., remote work).
Updating a budget to reflect changed circumstances — income, expenses, goals, or life events.
Recalculating budget allocations when income increases (raise/new job) or decreases (job loss/retirement).
Adjusting finances for major changes: marriage (combined income), baby ($15K+/year), divorce, etc.
The ability to adapt financially to unexpected changes without crisis or excessive debt.
Strategy for handling large one-time income (inheritance, bonus, tax refund, lottery): save, invest, or pay debt.
System where higher income portions are taxed at higher rates; everyone pays the same rate on the same levels.
All taxable income before deductions: wages, salaries, interest, dividends, business income.
Gross income minus specific above-the-line deductions (student loan interest, IRA contributions, etc.).
A fixed amount subtracted from AGI before calculating tax ($14,600 single / $29,200 married filing jointly, 2024).
The amount actually subject to tax: AGI minus deductions (standard or itemized).
A range of income taxed at a specific rate in the progressive system.
The tax rate applied to the LAST dollar of income — the rate of the highest bracket you reach.
Total tax paid ÷ total income — the average rate actually paid across all brackets.
The total amount of federal (and state) income tax you owe for the year.
When inflation pushes income into higher brackets; brackets are adjusted annually for inflation to offset this.
Annual tax on real estate based on assessed value; funds local services (schools, fire, police, roads).
The value assigned to a property by the tax assessor for property tax calculation (may differ from market value).
Tax added to retail purchases at point of sale; varies by state and locality.
Federal tax on the transfer of assets at death; applies only above the exemption ($13.6M in 2024).
Tax on specific goods: gasoline (~$0.18/gallon federal + state), alcohol, tobacco, luxury items.
The regular payment (monthly/annually) you make to maintain insurance coverage.
The amount you pay out-of-pocket before insurance begins covering costs.
A fixed amount you pay for a covered service (e.g., $30 doctor visit, $10 prescription).
Pure death benefit for a specific term (10-30 years); no cash value; most affordable protection.
Replaces 60-70% of income if you're unable to work due to illness or injury — often the most overlooked coverage.
Eliminating a risk entirely by not engaging in the activity (don't own a boat = no boat liability).
Taking steps to decrease the probability or impact of a risk (security system, healthy habits).
Shifting financial risk to another party, typically through insurance (pay premium → insurer absorbs loss).
Accepting a risk and self-insuring — viable for small, manageable risks (choosing a high deductible).
Probability of an event × its financial impact; used to decide whether to insure against a risk.
If current tax rate > retirement rate: Traditional IRA wins. If current rate < retirement rate: Roth wins.
Selling investments at a loss to offset gains, reducing taxes. Net loss up to $3,000 can offset ordinary income.
Investments in Traditional IRA/401(k) grow without annual taxes; taxes owed on withdrawal (vs. taxable accounts).
Using realized losses to reduce or eliminate taxes on realized gains in the same year.
The tax savings from a deduction = deduction amount × marginal tax rate (e.g., $5,000 deduction at 22% saves $1,100).
Analyzing total costs across scenarios (healthy year vs. major illness) for different insurance options.
High-deductible health plan (HDHP) that qualifies for a Health Savings Account — triple tax advantage.
Calculating coverage amount based on income replacement, debts, education costs, and final expenses.
Steps to file and receive insurance benefits: document, report, file claim, adjuster review, settlement/payment.
Period without insurance coverage, or a situation not covered by your policy — identify and address gaps.
Free tax preparation software available for income ≤ $79,000; accesses IRS-approved software at no cost.
Employer-provided form showing annual wages and tax withholdings; needed for tax filing.
Various forms reporting non-wage income: 1099-INT (interest), 1099-DIV (dividends), 1099-NEC (freelance).
The main US individual income tax return form; all filers use this form.
The difference when you've overpaid taxes through withholding — it's YOUR money being returned, not a gift from the government.
Simplest business structure; owner has complete control but unlimited personal liability for all debts.
Business structure providing personal liability protection with pass-through taxation — combines benefits of corporation and sole proprietorship.
One-time expenses to launch a business: equipment, inventory, legal fees, licenses, marketing materials.
A formal document detailing business goals, strategies, market analysis, financial projections, and funding needs.
Fixed: don't change with output (rent, insurance). Variable: change with production volume (materials, labor per unit).
Total income from sales before any expenses are deducted (also called gross sales or top line).
Direct costs of producing the goods/services sold: materials, direct labor, manufacturing overhead.
Revenue minus COGS; shows profitability of core product/service before overhead.
Final profit after ALL expenses, including operating costs, interest, and taxes.
Net profit ÷ Revenue × 100; shows what percentage of each revenue dollar becomes profit.
The sales volume where total revenue = total costs; no profit or loss. The minimum sales needed to survive.
Selling price minus variable cost per unit; the amount each unit 'contributes' toward covering fixed costs.
Contribution margin ÷ selling price; the percentage of each dollar of revenue available to cover fixed costs.
Costs that remain constant regardless of production volume: rent, insurance, salaries, loan payments.
Current sales minus break-even sales; how much sales can drop before the business loses money.
Self-funding a business using personal savings and reinvesting early profits; maintains full ownership and control.
Small Business Administration-backed loan; government guarantee reduces bank risk, enabling favorable terms for small businesses.
Wealthy individual who invests personal money in startups, typically $25,000-$500,000, in exchange for equity and mentorship.
Professional investment firms providing $500K-$50M+ to high-growth startups in exchange for significant equity (20-50%+).
Equity: selling ownership shares (no repayment, but diluted control). Debt: borrowing money (repayment required, but you keep 100% ownership).
Typical startup $50-150K; lower overhead than restaurant; BEP often 300-500 meals/month depending on pricing and costs.
Low startup ($1-10K); very low fixed costs; variable costs include product COGS + shipping + platform fees.
Cash flow = actual money moving in/out. Profit = accounting measure. A business can be profitable but cash-poor (or vice versa).
(Net Profit ÷ Total Investment) × 100; measures how effectively invested capital generates profit.
Time to recover initial investment from profits; shorter = faster return of capital to the investor.
Total marketing spend ÷ number of new customers acquired; how much it costs to gain one customer.
Total revenue expected from a customer over the entire relationship; LTV should be ≥ 3× CAC for a healthy business.
Revenue generated ÷ advertising cost; a ROAS of 4:1 means $4 revenue per $1 spent on ads.
Percentage of visitors/leads who complete a desired action (purchase, sign-up); typically 2-5% for e-commerce.
(Revenue from Marketing − Marketing Cost) ÷ Marketing Cost × 100; measures overall marketing effectiveness.
Risk from changing customer demand, new competitors, or economic conditions affecting sales.
Risk from cash flow shortfalls, overleveraging (too much debt), or interest rate increases.
Risk from internal processes: supply chain disruption, equipment failure, key employee departure.
Spreading risk across multiple products, markets, or revenue streams to reduce dependence on any single source.
Policies protecting against specific risks: liability, property, workers' comp, business interruption, cyber liability.
Leading small business accounting software; tracks income, expenses, invoicing, and generates financial reports automatically.
Typically 2.6-2.9% + $0.30 per transaction for credit card processing (Square, Stripe); a cost of doing business.
Real-time visual display of key financial metrics (revenue, expenses, cash flow, profit) for quick business health assessment.
Online-based accounting systems accessible from anywhere; automatic backups, real-time collaboration, and bank feed integration.
Financial technology — innovations like digital payments, automated lending, blockchain, and AI-driven financial tools.
A share of ownership in a company; entitles the holder to a portion of profits (dividends) and voting rights.
Share price × total shares outstanding; classifies companies as large-cap (>$10B), mid-cap, or small-cap (<$2B).
A portion of company profits distributed to shareholders, usually quarterly; expressed as yield (annual dividend ÷ price).
Period of rising stock prices (generally 20%+ sustained increase); associated with economic optimism.
Period of falling stock prices (20%+ decline from recent highs); associated with recession fears.
Short identifier for a publicly traded company (e.g., AAPL = Apple, MSFT = Microsoft, GOOGL = Alphabet).
Price-to-Earnings ratio; stock price ÷ earnings per share. Higher P/E suggests higher growth expectations.
Net income ÷ shares outstanding; fundamental measure of company profitability on a per-share basis.
Number of shares traded in a given period; high volume = high interest/liquidity.
Highest and lowest prices over the past year; provides context for current price level.
Index of 500 large US companies weighted by market cap; covers ~80% of US stock market value; primary benchmark.
Index of 30 large US companies weighted by stock price; oldest widely followed US index (since 1896).
A fund that passively tracks an index (like S&P 500); low fees because no active stock picking is needed.
A standard for measuring investment performance (e.g., 'My fund returned 12% vs. S&P 500's 10%').
Index weighting method where larger companies (higher market cap) have proportionally greater influence on the index.
Long-term strategy of purchasing quality investments and holding through market ups and downs; lowest cost, historically very effective.
Strategy of finding stocks trading below their intrinsic value (low P/E, P/B); pioneered by Benjamin Graham and Warren Buffett.
Strategy focused on companies with high revenue/earnings growth, often with higher P/E ratios; accepts higher valuations for faster growth.
Investing a fixed dollar amount at regular intervals regardless of price; reduces timing risk and emotional decision-making.
Dividing investments among asset classes (stocks, bonds, cash, real estate) based on risk tolerance, goals, and time horizon.
The price of one currency expressed in another (e.g., 1 USD = 0.92 EUR means $1 buys €0.92).
When a currency gains value relative to another (stronger dollar means it buys more foreign currency).
When a currency loses value relative to another (weaker dollar means it buys less foreign currency).
Theory that exchange rates should adjust so identical goods cost the same across countries in the long run.
Investment by a company or individual in one country into business interests in another country (factories, offices).
A rapid, severe decline in stock prices (often 20%+ in days or weeks); caused by panic, economic shocks, or systemic failures.
The period needed for markets to return to pre-crash levels; historically ranges from months to years.
Study of psychological biases affecting financial decisions: panic selling, herd mentality, overconfidence, loss aversion.
Emotionally driven selling during market drops; often locks in losses at or near the bottom — the worst time to sell.
Tendency of markets to return to long-term averages over time; crashes are typically followed by recoveries.
Digital platform for buying/selling stocks, ETFs, and other securities; most major brokers now offer $0 commissions.
Automated investment platform that creates and manages a diversified portfolio based on your risk tolerance and goals; low fees (~0.25%).
Computer programs executing trades based on mathematical rules at speeds impossible for humans.
Ability to buy a portion of a share (e.g., $50 worth of a $3,000 stock); makes expensive stocks accessible to all investors.
Technology-driven innovation transforming financial services: mobile banking, peer-to-peer payments, cryptocurrency, automated investing.
The psychological tendency where losses feel ~2× worse than equivalent gains feel good; leads to panic selling.
Tendency to seek, interpret, and remember information that confirms pre-existing beliefs while ignoring contradictory evidence.
Fixating on a reference point (like purchase price) when making decisions, even when it's irrelevant to current value.
Overestimating one's ability to predict markets or pick winners; leads to excessive trading and concentrated positions.
Anxiety about missing profitable opportunities; drives buying at peaks (when 'everyone' is talking about a stock).
Core finance principle: money available now is worth more than the same amount in the future due to its earning potential.
The current worth of a future sum of money discounted at a given rate; answers 'What is $X in N years worth today?'
The value of a current amount after earning interest for N periods; FV = PV × (1+r)^n.
The interest rate used to determine present value; reflects opportunity cost and risk.
A series of equal payments made at regular intervals (mortgage payments, retirement withdrawals, insurance premiums).
Sum of all discounted future cash flows minus the initial investment; measures whether an investment creates value.
The required rate of return used to discount future cash flows; often the cost of capital or opportunity cost.
The net money flowing in or out during each period; includes revenue minus expenses.
NPV > 0: accept (creates value). NPV < 0: reject (destroys value). NPV = 0: break-even return.
The process of evaluating and selecting long-term investments using NPV, IRR, and payback period.
The discount rate that makes NPV exactly zero; represents the project's annualized effective return rate.
The minimum acceptable rate of return for an investment; IRR must exceed this for the project to be accepted.
IRR gives a rate of return (percentage); NPV gives a dollar value. NPV is generally preferred for conflicting projects.
Modified IRR that assumes reinvestment at the cost of capital (more realistic than IRR's reinvestment assumption).
Traditional method for finding IRR by testing discount rates until NPV ≈ 0; modern tools (Excel) calculate instantly.
A spreadsheet-based tool that projects financial performance using assumptions, formulas, and scenarios.
Testing best case, base case, and worst case assumptions to understand the range of possible outcomes.
Changing ONE variable at a time to see its impact on the output (e.g., how does profit change if revenue drops 10%?).
Projected/forecasted financial statements (income statement, balance sheet, cash flow) based on assumptions.
Advanced modeling using thousands of random scenarios to produce probability distributions of outcomes.
The proportion of debt vs. equity a company uses for financing; affects risk, cost of capital, and returns.
The blended rate: (% debt × cost of debt after tax) + (% equity × cost of equity); the minimum return for new projects.
Cost of equity = Risk-free rate + Beta × (Market return − Risk-free rate); relates required return to risk.
Measure of a stock's volatility relative to the market; β=1 moves with market, β>1 more volatile, β<1 less volatile.
Using debt to amplify returns (and risks); measured by debt-to-equity ratio.
A measure of investment return volatility; higher σ = more dispersion around the average return = more risk.
(Portfolio return − risk-free rate) ÷ standard deviation; measures excess return per unit of risk. Higher is better.
The maximum expected loss over a given period at a specified confidence level (e.g., '95% VaR of $50,000' means 95% chance of losing less than $50K).
Measure of how two investments move relative to each other; −1 (opposite) to +1 (perfectly together). Low correlation = better diversification.
Return compared to the risk taken; two investments with the same return but different risk levels have different risk-adjusted returns.
GDP growth drives corporate earnings; recessions (negative GDP) trigger bear markets. Strong GDP = bullish for stocks.
Inflation erodes real returns. At 3% inflation, a 7% nominal return yields only ~4% real return.
US central bank controlling monetary policy: setting interest rates (federal funds rate) and managing money supply.
Fed actions to control money supply and interest rates: raising rates slows economy/inflation; lowering rates stimulates growth.
Stock prices are determined by supply/demand: more buyers than sellers = price rises; more sellers = price falls.
The risk that exchange rate changes will reduce the value of international investments when converted back to the investor's home currency.
Using forward contracts, options, or currency-hedged ETFs to protect against unfavorable exchange rate movements.
Developing countries with rapidly growing economies (China, India, Brazil); higher potential returns but higher risk.
Borrowing in a low-interest-rate currency and investing in a high-interest-rate currency; profitable until rates or exchange rates shift.
The risk that a foreign government defaults on debt, imposes capital controls, or makes policy changes that harm investments.
Future value formula: how much a present sum grows with compound interest over n periods at rate r.
Present value formula: today's equivalent of a future sum, discounted at rate r for n periods.
Break-even units = Fixed Costs ÷ Contribution Margin per unit (Price minus Variable Cost).
Net Present Value: sum of all discounted future cash flows minus the initial investment.
Sharpe ratio: excess return per unit of risk; higher = better risk-adjusted performance.
Show all work, label formulas, state assumptions, explain reasoning, and connect math to economic principles.
A problem requiring sequential application of multiple concepts (e.g., calculate after-tax return, then find PV, then compare NPV).
Converting between annual/monthly/daily rates and periods: monthly rate = annual rate ÷ 12; periods = years × 12.
After calculating, verify the answer makes sense (e.g., PV should be less than FV; higher risk should mean higher return).
Connecting mathematical results to real-world economic implications and decision-making in AP responses.
Making decisions by comparing marginal benefit to marginal cost — a core principle unifying economics and finance.
Price elasticity of demand determines pricing power: inelastic goods (necessities) can bear price increases; elastic goods cannot.
Monetary: Fed controls rates/money supply. Fiscal: Congress controls spending/taxes. Both impact financial markets.
The degree to which market prices reflect all available information — connected to both micro and macro analysis.
GDP, unemployment, CPI, consumer confidence — data that drives financial market movements and investment decisions.
Comparing total cost of renting (rent + renter's insurance) vs. buying (mortgage, taxes, insurance, maintenance, opportunity cost of down payment) over time.
Purchase price + insurance + gas + maintenance + depreciation − resale value = true cost of a vehicle over ownership period.
(Lifetime earnings with degree − lifetime earnings without − total college cost) / total college cost × 100.
Using FV of annuity formula to determine monthly savings needed to reach a target retirement nest egg.
Guideline that retirees can withdraw 4% of their portfolio in year 1 (adjusting for inflation) with high probability of money lasting 30+ years.
Total assets minus total liabilities; your financial 'score' — track it quarterly to measure progress.
50% needs (rent, food, insurance), 30% wants (entertainment, dining), 20% savings/debt payoff.
Debt payoff strategy: pay minimums on all debts, put extra money toward the HIGHEST interest rate debt first.
Debt payoff strategy: pay off smallest balances first for psychological wins (higher total interest cost than avalanche).
Legal documents ensuring your assets are distributed according to your wishes: will, beneficiaries, POA, healthcare directive.
The ability to understand and effectively use financial skills: budgeting, investing, borrowing, taxation, and personal financial management.
Interest calculated on both the initial principal AND accumulated interest — the most powerful force in finance.
Spreading investments across asset classes, sectors, and geographies to reduce risk without proportionally reducing expected returns.
Structuring financial activities to legally minimize taxes: retirement accounts, asset location, loss harvesting, and strategic deductions.
Assess current position → set goals → create strategy → implement plan → monitor and adjust — continuous cycle.
Applying multiple financial concepts simultaneously to a realistic life situation requiring integrated analysis.
1. Identify the decision. 2. Gather data. 3. Calculate options. 4. Compare quantitatively. 5. Consider qualitative factors. 6. Decide.
Optimization: finding the mathematically best solution. Satisficing: finding a 'good enough' solution that meets all constraints.
Considering all financial dimensions — income, taxes, debt, savings, insurance, and opportunity costs — simultaneously.
Small decisions compound dramatically: a $100/month savings difference at 22 vs. not starting = ~$350K+ difference by retirement.
